scenario: You raised pre-flop with A♣K♦ and c-bet. Board K♠7♥2♣. You have top pair top kicker.
question: Which Hardin hand-strength category is this, and what's the default plan?
options: Strong value — bet 1-3 streets for value
Medium strength — check down for showdown
explanation: Top pair top kicker on a dry board is a STRONG VALUE hand. Hardin's default: bet for value across multiple streets — value betting is where most micro-stakes profit comes from. You want worse Kx, pairs, and draws to pay you off.

scenario: You raised with A♠J♣, c-bet. Board J♦8♠5♥ — but a tight player called your flop bet and the turn brings a 2. You have top pair, decent kicker.
question: Category and default line on the turn against a tight caller?
options: Medium strength — pot control, check or thin value vs weak players only
explanation: Top pair with an okay (not top) kicker is MEDIUM STRENGTH. Hardin: usually check these down for showdown value, thin-value-bet only vs weaker opponents heads-up. Against a tight player who called your flop bet, their range is firming up — don't bloat the pot with a one-pair hand. Pot control.

scenario: You raised with Q♠J♠, c-bet. Board T♠9♦4♣. You have an open-ended straight draw + backdoor flush + two overcards.
question: Category and default line?
options: Draw — semi-bluff with good equity + fold equity + barrel potential
explanation: This is a premium DRAW: 8 outs to the straight, two overcards, backdoor flush. Hardin: semi-bluff when you have drawing equity + fold equity + barrel potential — all three are present. You can bet the flop and credibly double-barrel many turns (any spade, K, 8, Q, J). Strong semi-bluff.

scenario: You raised with A♥5♥, c-bet. Board K♣9♠4♦ (you have ace-high, no pair, no draw). A calling station called your flop c-bet.
question: Category and line against a calling station?
options: Weak hand — check/fold, don't keep firing at a station
explanation: Ace-high with no pair and no draw is a WEAK hand. Hardin: weak-hand play is situational — c-bet bluff only vs high-fold-to-cbet opponents. Against a CALLING STATION (who never folds), firing again is lighting money on fire. Check/fold unless you improve. The player type determines the line.

scenario: YOUR HAND. You called a 3-bet with K♠T♠. Board 6♦Q♣J♠ — you have K-high with a gutshot (any A makes Broadway). Villain (TAG 3-bettor) bets.
question: Honestly categorize K-high+gutshot vs a 3-bettor's range here.
options: Weak/marginal draw — only 4 outs to a gutshot, no pair, vs a strong capped-up range; check/fold-ish
explanation: Your real hand. K-high with only a gutshot (4 outs) against a TAG's QQ+/AK 3-betting range is a WEAK/marginal holding, not a real draw. You have no pair, no showdown value, and minimal equity vs a range that smashed Q-J-6. This is the hand you lost ~97 BB with by continuing. Correctly categorizing it as weak = fold to the flop bet and move on.

scenario: You raise, BB calls. Flop K♦Q♠5♣. You hold K♣J♠ (top pair, J kicker). BB check-calls flop, check-calls turn, then LEADS big on a 2♥ river.
question: Top pair decent kicker, but BB suddenly leads the river. Re-categorize your hand NOW.
options: Now a bluff-catcher — passive player leading after calling twice has KQ/sets/two-pair; your TPGK demotes to medium-at-best; lean fold to big lead
explanation: Hand strength is dynamic — it changes street by street with the action. KJ was strong on the flop, but a passive caller who suddenly leads big on the river has upgraded their range to hands that beat one pair (KQ two pair, sets, slowplayed monsters). Your TPGK demotes to a bluff-catcher, and at micro a passive river lead is rarely a bluff. Recategorize and lean fold. This is the exact discipline that plugs your biggest leak.

scenario: You raise with 8♠8♦, one caller. Flop 8♥4♣2♦ — you flopped a set (three eights) on a dry board.
question: Category and default plan with a set on a dry board?
options: Strong value (monster) — bet/build the pot for stacks; occasionally slowplay one street on very dry boards
explanation: A set is a STRONG VALUE monster. Default: bet and build the pot to play for stacks. On a very dry board (8-4-2) you can occasionally check ONE street to let them catch up or bluff, but the goal is getting stacks in. This is the opposite end from the one-pair trap — when you truly have it, maximize.

scenario: YOUR HAND. You 4-bet-call preflop... wait, you CALLED a 4-bet with A♣Q♣. Flop K-8-4 checks through. Turn blank, villain bets, you call. River pairs the board; you BET and villain shows QQ.
question: Against a 4-bettor (QQ+/AK), categorize your A-Q on K-8-4 and find the leak.
options: Weak vs this range — A-high beats nothing a 4-bettor continues with; betting river turns it into a bluff that only worse folds and better calls
explanation: Your real hand (−105 BB). A 4-bet range (QQ+/AK) crushes ace-high: every hand that continues beats you. A-Q on K-8-4 is a WEAK hand here — no pair, no draw, dominated. Betting the river is the cardinal error: worse hands (none exist in their range) won't call, better hands always do. Check-fold by the river. Correct categorization = recognizing ace-high is worthless vs a uniformly strong range.
